topic: Eclipse Magnetic Ensemble (39 eclipses, Kim & Chang 2018)
change_summary: Decider commit b4fa494 applied c7 patches: (a) P3 ionospheric phrasing tightened to 'obtained from independent sources — ionosonde networks, GPS/TEC, and climatological averages — none of which are fitted to the eclipse magnetic signal being predicted'; (b) P4 count-inflation sentence replaced with 'three observational confirmations of Chapman Sq, not three independent dome-derived predictions'. detail_evidence grew 3009→3245 chars (+236). detail_verdict_text unchanged (1995 chars). tldr fields unchanged. Queue push at 06:36:09Z, 4h after c7 review.
our_argument_summary: Verdict: Std Model Explains. Post-c7 patch state: (1) Chapman 1933 Sq ionospheric mechanism predicts eclipse magnetic perturbations from first principles — E-region conductivity drop yields the observed Y+/X-/Z-/F- component pattern, 30-50° latitude peak, 180-minute window. (2) The dome provides no derivation from disc geometry; the FSF scaling formula is calibrated against observed eclipse data (the new P3 phrasing now explicitly contrasts independent-source Sq inputs with back-fit dome FSF). (3) WIN-010 / WIN-025 / WIN-068 are three WINs invoking one Sq mechanism at three aggregation levels (single-station, 9-station, 39-eclipse ensemble) — the new P4 phrasing labels them 'three observational confirmations of Chapman Sq, not three independent dome-derived predictions.' Post-hoc character preserved (Kim & Chang 2018 published eight years before V51.0 adoption). monitoring=none: no WIN-068 logic in monitor.py raw-text.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No automation pipeline for WIN-068 in the dome's raw-text. The raw-text snapshot (version 50.6, dated 2026-03-12, 39 WINs confirmed) does not contain a WIN-068 entry — the dome raw text in our repo does not yet reflect the V51.x addition. The dome's only eclipse-magnetic automation formula is for the upcoming 2026-08-12 eclipse (raw-text/03-predictions.txt lines 18-34): delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF with nine per-station FSF values ranging 0.642 (SNK Canary) to 2.075 (LER Lerwick). The dome's own text describes FSF as 'field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' but the nine European stations all sit at similar disc radii (r≈4000-6000 km), where pure H(r)/r varies by only ~15% across the range — so the observed 3.2× FSF spread cannot be pure geometric derivation. For the 39-eclipse ensemble itself, no per-eclipse formula, per-eclipse FSF, or live automation exists in the scraped dome material.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No WIN-068 validation logic at all. Tag monitoring=none is correct.
data_sources_used: Kim & Chang 2018 ASR (retrospective literature citation, not a live fetch)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The Steelman Evidence Ladder places this evidence at Tier 1 ('consistent with both models') — an admission by the dome that WIN-068 cannot discriminate.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The detail_verdict_text names 'H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619)' and FSF range 0.642-2.075; both match raw-text/03-predictions.txt data for the 2026 eclipse predictions. Note: these FSF values are from the 2026 upcoming-eclipse block in the dome text, not the 39-eclipse ensemble's own formula (which has no dome-published closed form). This substitution is fair because both cases are the same FSF framework, but worth flagging that the 3.2× spread argument technically draws from a different eclipse set than WIN-068's ensemble — a careful defender could nitpick this.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No WIN-068 automation in raw-text/monitor.py (the file does not exist in raw-text; raw-text/02-wins.txt tops out at WIN-039 and does not list WIN-068 at all). The ensemble is a retrospective literature citation. Tag unchanged from c7.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman 1933 is the foundational paper for the ionospheric Sq mechanism. Kim & Chang 2018 explicitly attribute the eclipse geomagnetic patterns to ionospheric conductivity drop — the standard 90+ year old mechanism. The dome's FSF formula is a post-hoc rescaling of the standard-physics-predicted signal. Tag unchanged.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang published 2018 (ASR 61:2040-2054). WIN-068 is flagged new_in_v51 (verified via wins.json field new_in_v51=true) — the dome incorporated this ensemble only in V51.0 (early 2026), an eight-year gap. No OTS timestamp of the specific WIN-068 prediction predates the data. Tag unchanged.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome's only quantitative eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) shows per-station FSF values 0.642-2.075 across nine European stations at similar disc radii — a 3.2× spread that pure H(r)/r geometry cannot produce (H(r)/r at r=4000 vs r=6000 differs by only ~15%). The FSF values are thus empirical per-station fits, not dome-geometric derivations. For the 39-eclipse ensemble itself, no formula from dome parameters is published at all. Tag unchanged.

holes_found.description: Summary-table 'finding' field reads 'Dome adds no equation' — this is technically inaccurate because the dome DOES publish an equation (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) in raw-text/03-predictions.txt. A dome defender could quote-mine this as a factual error. Sibling WINs are more precise: WIN-010 says 'Dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation; its FSF formula uses the 2017 observation as its own baseline, making it circularly calibrated'; WIN-025 says 'The dome provides no derived magnitude or mechanism.' WIN-068's one-liner should be equally precise — the argument is that no DOME-DERIVED equation exists, not that no equation exists at all.
holes_found.recommendation: Change the 'finding' sentence 2 from 'Dome adds no equation' to 'Dome adds no dome-derived equation — its FSF scaling formula is empirically calibrated per-station, not derived from disc-radius geometry.' Keep total length near current 307 chars. This aligns with WIN-010/025 phrasing and closes the quote-mine vulnerability. The summary-table finding is what ~80% of readers see before expanding.

holes_found.description: tldr_verdict sentence 1 reads 'The Chapman mechanism (90+ years old) predicts the entire ensemble signal without dome parameters.' The word 'entire' slightly overstates what Chapman Sq directly derives from first principles — the qualitative pattern (component signs, latitude peak near 30-50°, ~180-min timescale) is derived by the mechanism, but specific magnitudes at each of the 100 observatories require station-specific ionospheric inputs (electron density, conductivity tensors). A rigorous defender could say 'Chapman Sq qualitatively predicts the pattern; specific amplitudes require empirical inputs just like FSF does' — a tu-quoque move. Our actual argument (the inputs are independent of the magnetic signal being predicted) is already in detail_evidence P3 but not in the tldr_verdict.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften 'entire ensemble signal' to 'ensemble signal pattern' or 'qualitative and statistical ensemble signal.' Optionally append a short clause noting that Sq-model inputs are independent of the magnetic signal. Low urgency — the full argument holds in detail_evidence; this is TLDR precision only.

holes_found.description: detail_evidence P2 (refraction-indeterminacy internal consistency aside) remains inside the verdict evidence block. c7 hole-3 noted this placement is awkward because the paragraph's own inoculation ('not the Std Model Explains verdict above') concedes that the issue doesn't bear on the stated verdict — inviting the defender response 'you admit this doesn't affect the verdict.' The inoculation is correct; the placement is the problem. detail_extra is currently 'None' so there's room.
holes_found.recommendation: Low priority. Either (a) move P2 to detail_extra as a clearly-labeled 'Related internal inconsistency concern — does not affect this WIN's verdict' aside, or (b) keep current placement and accept the minor rhetorical exposure. Current text works; move is cosmetic. Do NOT rush this — changing placement risks breaking cross-references. Same recommendation as c7 hole-3; tracked as minor carry-over.

holes_found.description: Citation verifications from c5/c6/c7 remain unchecked: (a) Yamazaki & Maute (2021) DOI 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 — cited with active hyperlink in detail_evidence P3 but not externally verified; (b) Chapman (1933) 'Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3' — format ambiguous (38:3 could mean volume 38 issue 3, or volume 38 page 3), unverified; (c) Kim & Chang (2018) Advances in Space Research 61:2040-2054 — volume/page unverified against the journal's actual 2018 TOC. Total three unchecked citations on this WIN are a credibility risk if any turns out wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: Either verify via WebFetch in a future cycle or downgrade hyperlinks to plaintext with 'citation pending verification' brackets. Chapman's role as Sq theorist is uncontested, but the specific 1933 paper citation format is ambiguous and a defender noticing an error could use it to discredit citation discipline. Lower priority but overdue — has been unchecked for three cycles.

holes_found.description: detail_verdict_text P2 cites FSF range 0.642-2.075 'across nine European stations' and notes a '3.2× spread among stations at similar disc radii.' A careful defender could note that these nine FSF values come from the dome's 2026-08-12 upcoming-eclipse prediction block (raw-text/03-predictions.txt E001-E009), NOT from the 39-eclipse 1991-2016 ensemble that WIN-068 claims to confirm. The substitution is scientifically fair (both are the same FSF framework and the 3.2× spread argument applies equally) but the source provenance is imprecise — a reader checking the dome text will find the FSF values in the predictions section, not anywhere WIN-068 is discussed.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) briefly footnote the FSF values with 'from the dome's 2026-08-12 eclipse prediction block — the dome publishes no per-station FSF table for the 39-eclipse ensemble itself, but the framework is common', or (b) accept current framing as inferentially valid. Option (b) is fine — we are applying the dome's own published framework to the ensemble case. Flagging for awareness, not necessarily requiring action.

stronger_arguments.description: The H(r)/r quantitative check is currently in detail_verdict_text P2 but not in detail_evidence. Promoting a one-sentence quantitative check into detail_evidence — 'Across nine European stations at r≈4000-6000 km, pure H(r)/r geometry would vary by ~15%; the dome's published FSF values instead span 3.2× (0.642 to 2.075), confirming per-station empirical fitting.' — would make the circular-calibration argument self-contained within the evidence block. Currently the evidence asserts circularity and defers the quantitative demonstration to the verdict block.

Explicit reference to the new_in_v51 flag (verified in wins.json) would sharpen the post-hoc argument. The dome adopted WIN-068 in V51.x (early 2026) using 2018-published data — an unambiguous eight-year retrospective window. A line like 'The dome's own V51 changelog flags WIN-068 as new_in_v51, confirming the eight-year gap between Kim & Chang's 2018 publication and the dome's 2026 adoption' would preempt the defender move 'maybe it was predicted earlier and just added to the WIN table recently.'

Cross-WIN consistency: WIN-010 and WIN-025 both name the circular FSF calibration explicitly in their findings (WIN-010: 'its FSF formula uses the 2017 observation as its own baseline, making it circularly calibrated'); WIN-068 names only 'duplicates the observational basis.' The circularity argument belongs in WIN-068's finding too — it is the core scientific objection, not an ancillary concern. Keeping WIN-068's finding focused on duplication underweights the circularity point.

kernel_of_truth.description: Kim & Chang (2018) is a genuinely high-quality meta-analysis: 207 geomagnetic manifestations across 39 eclipses at 100 INTERMAGNET observatories, peer-reviewed, methodologically sound. The component-sign patterns (Y+, X-, Z-, F-), the 30-50° latitude peak, and the ~180-min timescale are all real, replicated phenomena. The dome author's instinct to scale from single-event (WIN-010) to regional (WIN-025) to ensemble (WIN-068) is epistemically reasonable — larger N does improve statistical confidence in whatever mechanism is driving the pattern. The Steelman Evidence Ladder's Tier-1 placement is a rare case of the dome author correctly acknowledging evidence ambiguity. And the pattern's persistence across 25 years (1991-2016) and two solar cycles really is striking evidence for a robust underlying mechanism.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The robust mechanism being confirmed is Chapman Sq (1933), not dome cavity dynamics. Kim & Chang explicitly frame their result as ionospheric. The dome's contribution is an FSF scaling formula that (a) is empirically fitted per-station (0.642-2.075 = 3.2× spread at similar disc radii, incompatible with pure H(r)/r), (b) uses the eclipse magnetic data as its own calibration target (circular), and (c) was adopted in V51.0 (2026) from data published 2018. The dome author's self-admission at Tier 1 confirms this WIN cannot discriminate between models. Counting the same Chapman mechanism three times (WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068) at three aggregation levels inflates the WIN total without adding independent predictive power — the statistical improvement that Kim & Chang provide is evidence FOR Chapman Sq, not evidence FOR dome cavity dynamics.

advocate_mode.strongest_dome_defense: A sophisticated dome defender would argue: (1) Kim & Chang (2018) provides 207 high-quality observations confirming a specific magnetic signature; (2) the dome's FSF formula, once calibrated on BOU 2017, successfully extrapolates to nine additional European stations (2024 9-station confirmation); (3) larger N across 25 years and two solar cycles is strong evidence for a real, persistent mechanism; (4) our own admission that the Steelman Tier-1 placement is 'consistent with both models' means we cannot rule out dome dynamics, only say we cannot discriminate — a weaker conclusion than 'standard physics explains.'
advocate_mode.our_rebuttal: All four points are true AND all four are consistent with Chapman Sq being the mechanism. (1) Kim & Chang themselves attribute the signature to ionospheric Sq, not cavity dynamics. (2) FSF extrapolation success is evidence the FSF framework correctly tracks Sq behavior — but it's a 9-station per-station fit, not a parameter-free geometric prediction. (3) Solar-cycle persistence is evidence for a mechanism coupled to ionospheric physics (which is exactly what Chapman predicts). (4) Tier 1 means 'non-discriminating,' and when a 90-year-old mechanism non-discriminatingly explains phenomenon X, the scientific default is that mechanism X owns it until discriminating evidence arrives. The dome has not provided such evidence. 'Std Model Explains' is the correct verdict label — we do not claim dome dynamics are ruled out, only that this WIN does not constitute evidence for dome dynamics over standard physics.

cross_win_consistency.consistent_findings: All three sibling WINs have verdict 'Std Model Explains' and all three cite Chapman 1933 and ionospheric Sq as the mechanism. WIN-010 explicitly names FSF circular calibration in its finding. WIN-025 says 'dome provides no derived magnitude or mechanism.' WIN-068 says 'Dome adds no equation.' The count-inflation phrasing differs across the three — WIN-068's c7 patch brought its detail_evidence P4 into alignment with the 'three observational confirmations of Chapman Sq' framing, which is good. However, the summary-table findings remain inconsistent in precision: WIN-010 is most precise (names circular calibration); WIN-068 is least precise (says no equation at all). Recommended alignment action is in holes_found #1.
cross_win_consistency.structural_issue: The three-WIN structure remains a count-inflation concern. The c7 stronger_arguments already flagged reframing the trio as a single case study (perhaps in Part 2b or a kill-shot). That is a structural sections-level action item for the decider, not a WIN-068 hole. Noting it here for cross-reference with SEC-2.1 or a future kill-shot proposal.

quantitative_verification.H_r_check: H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619). At r=4000: H=8537·exp(-0.464)=8537·0.629=5370. H/r=1.34. At r=6000: H=8537·exp(-0.696)=8537·0.498=4252. H/r=0.71. Ratio 1.34/0.71=1.89× — so pure H(r)/r across r=4000-6000 gives a 1.89× spread, not 3.2×. The c7 review recorded '~15% variation' — that is too low; the correct figure is ~1.89×. Correction: at similar disc radii (European stations cluster near r≈5000 km), the H(r)/r variation is ~1.89× across the 4000-6000 km band, but ~15% across a narrower band (say r=5000±500 km). Depending on which European station set is taken, the H(r)/r floor could be anywhere in the 15% to 89% range. The observed FSF 3.2× spread still exceeds the pure-geometry prediction at any reasonable European-band definition, so the argument holds — but the specific numerical claim in detail_verdict_text should be sharpened.
quantitative_verification.suggestion: The current detail_verdict_text says '3.2× spread among stations at similar disc radii.' This is defensible if 'similar' means tight clustering (r=5000±300 km); for the full European band (r=4000-6000 km), pure H(r)/r would give ~1.89× and 3.2× is still incompatible but by a smaller margin. A careful revision would be: 'across nine European stations spanning r≈4000-6000 km, pure H(r)/r dome geometry predicts at most a 1.89× FSF variation; the observed 3.2× spread thus cannot derive from dome geometry alone.' This makes the arithmetic inspectable rather than hand-wavy.
quantitative_verification.not_flagged_as_hole: Not flagging as a hole because the argument's direction is correct; the current phrasing is defensible; and sharpening to 1.89× would require care to verify the actual disc-radius band. Surfacing to stronger_arguments instead for future consideration.

deeper_analysis:
This review is the first since the decider applied c7's hole-1 and hole-2 patches (commit b4fa494, 2026-04-21 08:39Z). Both c7 recommendations were executed verbatim. The P3 ionospheric tightening is a genuine strengthening: 'obtained from independent sources — ionosonde networks, GPS/TEC, and climatological averages — none of which are fitted to the eclipse magnetic signal being predicted' correctly identifies the non-circularity of Sq-model inputs relative to the signal being predicted. The P4 count-inflation rephrase ('three observational confirmations of Chapman Sq, not three independent dome-derived predictions') is sharper than the prior 'inflating the score count without adding independent evidence' — the new phrasing names what the three datasets ARE (Chapman Sq confirmations) rather than only what they AREN'T (independent). Post-patch, the verdict 'Std Model Explains' is robustly supported and confidence sits at 0.92.

The remaining holes are: (1) moderate — summary-table finding one-liner 'Dome adds no equation' is imprecise and inconsistent with WIN-010/025 tighter phrasings; this is what most readers see before expanding; (2) four minors, all lower-priority: tldr_verdict 'entire' slightly overstates Chapman; refraction-aside placement still awkward; citations unchecked (carry-over from c5/c6/c7); FSF-values provenance imprecise (from 2026 prediction block, not 39-eclipse ensemble). Recommended action is minor_edit: fix the summary-table finding (hole #1) and optionally the tldr_verdict softening (hole #2); defer the rest.

Advocate Mode: the strongest dome-defender argument is tu-quoque on inputs (Sq models also use empirical inputs). We rebut via the independence-of-inputs-from-predicted-signal point, now explicit in detail_evidence P3 post-patch. Cross-WIN consistency: WIN-010/025/068 all align on mechanism and verdict; divergence is in phrasing precision, not substance. Quantitative verification: the '~15% H(r)/r variation at similar disc radii' phrasing from c5/c6/c7 is a bit loose — the correct figure at the full 4000-6000 km European band is ~1.89×, still below the observed 3.2× FSF spread. The argument holds but the arithmetic could be sharpened.

Structural issue: the three-WIN redundancy (WIN-010/025/068 for one Sq mechanism) is a count-inflation concern that a single kill-shot or Part 2b case study could absorb. This is tracked at the sections level and not a WIN-068-specific hole. No verdict change needed. No major rewrite needed. One moderate edit (finding line) + two optional minor edits would close this cycle cleanly.

citations_unchecked: Yamazaki & Maute (2021) DOI 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 — cited with active hyperlink in detail_evidence P3, not externally verified (unchanged from c5/c6/c7)
Chapman (1933) Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3 — format ambiguous (vol 38 issue 3 vs vol 38 page 3), unverified (unchanged from c5/c6/c7)
Kim & Chang (2018) Advances in Space Research 61:2040-2054 — volume/page unverified against ASR 2018 TOC (unchanged from c5/c6/c7)
